claim: Individual affective dispositions partially cause the stability and direction of job-related evaluations across settings.
Learners resolve the infinite possible word meanings by using structure-dependent priors that bias toward natural kinds and taxonomic levels.
Emotional responses arise from comparing events to mentally recruited alternatives, not from objective event properties.
Knowing an outcome reduces ability to imagine alternatives, making that outcome appear inevitable rather than contingent.
Confidence judgments at extreme levels systematically overestimate accuracy, persisting even with training and incentives.
People use own feelings to predict abstract others' risk preferences but project their own preferences onto concrete individuals.
